findings.description: Pagination off-by-one: docstring says `page` is 1-based and default is page=1, but `offset = page * page_size` skips the first page_size rows, so page=1 returns rows 21-40 and the entire first page of results is permanently unreachable; correct formula is `(page - 1) * page_size`.

No lower-bound validation on `page`/`page_size`: a negative `page` produces a negative slice start (e.g. offset=-20) that silently returns a nonsensical tail window instead of erroring, and `page_size<=0` (min() only clamps the upper bound) yields an always-empty or garbage window.

Caller-supplied `name_pattern` is passed straight to `re.compile` with no try/except, so an invalid regex (e.g. '[', '(', '*') raises an unhandled re.error and crashes the request instead of returning a handled validation error. (Latent ReDoS/backtracking is a security/perf concern - defer detail to those critics.)

`matcher.search(u['display_name'])` and the sort key `u['display_name'].lower()` (line 62) assume `display_name` is always a non-null string; a NULL value in that column (SQLite columns are nullable by default) makes dict() yield None and raises TypeError/AttributeError. Conditional on schema, which is out of scope.

Unfiltered search fetchall()s the entire non-deleted users table into memory and filters/sorts/paginates in Python (no SQL LIMIT/OFFSET/ORDER BY); MAX_PAGE_SIZE gives a false sense of bounded work while the underlying fetch is unbounded. Defer perf detail to critic-performance.
`departments()` returns DISTINCT department values which can include a NULL row as a `None` element, violating the declared `list[str]` return type if any user has a NULL department.

`deactivate` uses `rowcount == 1` and lacks `AND deleted = 0`, so re-deactivating an already-deleted user still returns True (row matched) while a nonexistent id returns False, conflating 'already inactive' and 'success' and giving no distinct signal for idempotent re-deactivation.
